title: Raghav Sethi — 60-Day Beast Mode Roadmap

resume_analysis.header: Brutal Resume Roast — Read This First
resume_analysis.blunt_truth: Raghav, you have a 9.0 CGPA, a hackathon win, a merit scholarship 3 times, and real SDE-1 work experience at 8 LPA. You are NOT a beginner. The startup shutting down is not a tragedy — it's a reset button at the perfect time. But here's where I'm going to be brutally honest with you. Because sugarcoating this will cost you lakhs in your next package.
gaps: React Native (Expo) with hooks, 50+ flows, component architecture

You built screens. You have NOT demonstrated React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, Suspense, concurrent features, rendering optimization — the stuff seniors live in. Expo abstracts so much that you may not know the raw DOM-level React.
Listed as a technology, used in PharmaSync
You used it as a "fancy React router with pages." You have zero demonstrated knowledge of ISR, RSC, Edge Runtime, streaming SSR, or Next.js App Router architecture. Interviewers WILL probe this.

Your resume has zero evidence of advanced TypeScript — generics, conditional types, discriminated unions, utility types, type inference. This is a MASSIVE red flag for senior roles. Mid-level devs use TS as "typed JavaScript." Seniors USE the type system.
Reduced upload latency with S3 direct upload
This is ONE performance win. You have no evidence of Core Web Vitals knowledge, bundle optimization, virtualization, Profiler usage, or systematic performance engineering. Senior = you own perf.
You used useState and hooks. "Hook-based state management" is not an architecture — it's a description. Senior engineers design state systems. You need Zustand/Jotai/TanStack Query patterns cold.
Zero test code shown anywhere in your projects. This is embarrassing for a senior candidate. Every serious company will give you a take-home with tests or ask about testing philosophy.
Listed, not demonstrated. WhatsApp Business API webhooks ≠ real-time UI engineering. You need WebSocket state management, reconnection logic, optimistic UI, and conflict resolution.

You have zero frontend system design evidence. Senior frontend interviews include: design a rich text editor, design an infinite feed, design a collaborative whiteboard. This is your biggest blindspot.
strengths: ✅ You shipped real production code (not just tutorials). The S3 multipart pipeline, WhatsApp webhook architecture, and n8n workflows show you can build end-to-end.
✅ 9.0 CGPA means you can learn fast. This roadmap is going to be brutal and you will handle it.
✅ You have MCA from PES University — better brand than most bootcamp grads applying to same roles.
✅ You already know React Native. That's a bonus skill that gives you hybrid mobile+web positioning.
✅ You understand async patterns, file handling, auth flows — backend-adjacent skills that make frontend engineers dangerous.

⚡ BOTTOM LINE: You are a solid mid-level developer with the capacity to become a very strong senior in 60 days IF you stop building surface-level features and start mastering the deep internals. Let's go.

months.title: React Mastery & Frontend Architecture
months.objective: By end of Month 1: You can open any production React codebase and immediately understand every architectural decision. You can explain Fiber, reconciliation, rendering optimization, TypeScript generics, and state architecture to a senior panel. You stop being a React user and become a React expert.

weeks.title: JavaScript Engine Room + React Fiber Internals
weeks.overview: Most React devs skip this. Most React devs are stuck at mid-level. These are the foundations that make everything else make sense. ZERO shortcuts.

days.title: Closures, Prototype Chain, Event Loop (The JS Internals React Lives On)
days.why_it_matters: Your hooks will produce stale closure bugs if you don't deeply understand this. The 'why does my useEffect have old state?' bug that 80% of mid-level devs cannot explain? It's a closure. Understand this cold.

topics.title: Closures & Lexical Scope
points: Definition: A function that retains access to its outer scope's variables even after the outer function has returned and its execution context is gone
Lexical scope: JS determines variable scope at WRITE TIME (where code is written), not at runtime
How closures capture by reference, not by value — this is the exact mechanism behind stale closures in hooks
Stale closure in useEffect: when your effect captures a value at mount and never updates it because deps array is wrong
Fix pattern 1: Add the variable to the dependency array so effect re-runs with fresh value
Fix pattern 2: useRef to hold mutable value that doesn't need to trigger re-render but always has latest value
Fix pattern 3: Functional state update setCount(prev => prev + 1) bypasses stale closure entirely
Classic closure-in-loop bug: var creates one binding, let creates per-iteration binding — understand WHY
IIFE pattern for solving loop closure: (function(i){ ... })(i)
Memory implications: closure keeps entire outer scope alive — how this causes memory leaks in long-lived subscriptions

topics.title: Prototype Chain
points: Every object has a [[Prototype]] internal slot pointing to another object or null
Property lookup: checks own properties first, then walks prototype chain to Object.prototype, then returns undefined
Object.create(proto): creates new object with proto as its [[Prototype]]
The 4 this-binding rules: Default (undefined/global), Implicit (obj.method()), Explicit (call/apply/bind), New (constructor)
Arrow functions: lexically bind this — they inherit this from the enclosing scope, no own this
Why class components needed .bind(this) in constructor: regular method = implicit binding that breaks when passed as callback
Why hooks solved this: function components + hooks have no this context problems

topics.title: Event Loop, Microtasks, Macrotasks
points: Call stack: synchronous execution, LIFO, one operation at a time
Web APIs: setTimeout, fetch, DOM events — handled outside V8 by browser
Callback queue (macrotask queue): setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks
Microtask queue: Promise .then/.catch/.finally, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver
Execution order per iteration: All synchronous code → ALL microtasks → ONE macrotask → ALL microtasks again → repeat
React batching: React 17 only batched state updates inside React event handlers; React 18 batches ALL updates including setTimeout, Promises, native events
requestAnimationFrame: fires before next paint — React uses this for scheduling non-urgent work
Long task: any JS that blocks the main thread > 50ms — causes dropped frames and Input Delay (INP issues)

topics.title: Immutability & Structural Sharing
points: JS primitives are immutable by nature (strings, numbers, booleans)
Objects/arrays are mutable reference types — two variables can point to same object in memory
Why React requires immutable updates: React uses Object.is() shallow comparison to detect changes — mutating existing object means old === new, React skips render
Shallow clone: {...obj} or Object.assign({}, obj) — copies one level, nested objects still share reference
Deep clone: structuredClone() (modern), JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()) (old, has limitations), Lodash cloneDeep
Structural sharing in Immer: only creates new object nodes along the "path of change", everything else keeps same reference — O(log n) instead of O(n) for deep clones
Why structural sharing matters: enables undo/redo with memory efficiency, powers Redux Toolkit

knowledge_check: Write a useEffect that causes a stale closure bug with a counter. Show the bug, explain exactly WHY it happens mechanically (what the closure is capturing), then fix it 3 different ways.
What is the execution order of: Promise.resolve().then(()=>console.log("A")); setTimeout(()=>console.log("B"),0); console.log("C")? Explain with the exact event loop mechanics.
Why does React 18 automatic batching work in setTimeout but React 17 did not? What specific mechanism changed?
Given: const a = {x: {y: 1}}; const b = {...a}; b.x.y = 99; Does a.x.y change? Why? How do you prevent this?
Explain the 4 this-binding rules with one code example for each. Then show how arrow functions break rule 2.
What is structural sharing in Immer? Draw a tree diagram showing a state tree before and after one nested property change, showing which nodes are reused vs newly created.
Why do hooks not need .bind(this) while class component methods do? Answer in terms of how this is bound in each case.
What causes a memory leak with closures? Write a subscription example that leaks and then fix it with useEffect cleanup.
What is Object.is() and how does it differ from === ? Give 2 examples where they produce different results. Why does React use Object.is()?
Your app has a button click that calls setState 3 times. In React 17 how many renders happen? In React 18? Why the difference?

days.title: React Fiber Architecture (The Engine Under React)
days.why_it_matters: This is the topic that separates senior devs from mid-level devs in interviews. 'How does React work internally?' Most candidates say 'virtual DOM'. Wrong answer. The correct answer is Fiber. Know it cold.

topics.title: Why Fiber Was Built (The Problem with Stack Reconciler)
points: React 15 used recursive stack-based reconciler: once started, could NOT be interrupted
Problem: large component trees would block the main thread for 100ms+ causing dropped frames
Goal of Fiber: make rendering interruptible, pauseable, resumable, and prioritizable
Fiber = unit of work: every component = one fiber node. React can pause between fibers.

topics.title: Fiber Node Structure (What's Actually in a Fiber)
points: type: component type (function, class, or string like "div")
key: the key prop for reconciliation identity
stateNode: the actual DOM node or class instance
return: pointer to parent fiber
child: pointer to first child fiber
sibling: pointer to next sibling fiber
pendingProps / memoizedProps: props being processed vs props from last render
memoizedState: the hooks linked list is stored here (useState, useEffect, etc.)
effectTag / flags: marks what work needs to happen in commit phase (Placement, Update, Deletion)
lanes: priority level of pending work (SyncLane = urgent, DefaultLane = normal, IdleLane = low priority)

topics.title: The Two-Tree Architecture (Current vs Work-In-Progress)
points: React always maintains TWO fiber trees simultaneously
Current tree: the tree currently rendered on screen
Work-in-progress tree: the new tree being built during reconciliation
alternate pointer: each fiber in current points to its counterpart in work-in-progress
Double buffering: when reconciliation completes, work-in-progress becomes current atomically
Why: allows React to discard in-progress work without affecting what's on screen

topics.title: Render Phase vs Commit Phase
points: Render phase (Reconciliation): Pure computation — builds work-in-progress fiber tree, diffs against current tree. INTERRUPTIBLE. Can be paused, aborted, restarted. Runs your render function / component body.

Commit phase: Applies all DOM mutations, runs layout effects (useLayoutEffect), then passive effects (useEffect). SYNCHRONOUS — never interrupted. Split into 3 sub-phases: before mutation, mutation, layout.
Why render phase must be pure/free of side effects: React may run it multiple times in Concurrent Mode
Why useLayoutEffect fires synchronously after DOM mutation: it's in the commit phase
Why useEffect fires asynchronously after paint: it's scheduled after commit phase completes

topics.title: Priority Lanes in React 18
points: SyncLane: Discrete user input (click, keyboard) — must update immediately, no interruption
InputContinuousLane: Continuous input (scroll, drag, mouse move)
DefaultLane: Normal updates (data fetching, non-urgent state changes)
TransitionLane: Updates wrapped in startTransition — can be interrupted
IdleLane: Background work when browser is idle
How React decides priority: user interaction = high, startTransition = low
Concurrent Mode: high priority work can interrupt low priority work mid-render

knowledge_check: Explain what a Fiber node is and list 8 fields it contains. What does each field store?
Why was React's stack reconciler a problem? What exactly could it not do that Fiber can?
What is the difference between the render phase and commit phase? Which is interruptible? Why does this matter?
React maintains two fiber trees. What are they called and why? What is the alternate pointer?
What are "lanes" in React 18? List 4 different lane priorities and give a real UI example of when each is used.
Why must the render phase (component body / render function) be free of side effects? What happens in Concurrent Mode if it isn't?
Why does useLayoutEffect fire before useEffect even though both are "after render"? Trace the commit phase sequence.
Your component's render function runs 2-3 times without any state change in Strict Mode. Why does React do this and what does it help catch?
What is double buffering in React's fiber architecture? Why is it important?
A high-priority click event interrupts a startTransition update mid-render. What happens to the in-progress work? How does React handle this?

days.title: Reconciliation, Diffing Algorithm & The Key Prop

topics.title: React's Two Diffing Assumptions
points: Assumption 1: Two elements of different TYPES produce completely different trees. React will unmount the old tree entirely and mount a new one.
Assumption 2: The developer can hint at element stability across renders with the key prop.
Without these assumptions: O(n³) tree comparison algorithm (too slow for UI). With them: O(n) — React only compares at same tree level.

topics.title: Element Type Comparison Rules
points: Same type, same position: React updates the existing DOM node with new props — preserves DOM node, preserves state
Different type, same position: React unmounts old component (calls cleanup, destroys state), mounts new component from scratch
This is why conditionally rendering <Input> vs <textarea> resets form state completely
This is why switching between <ComponentA> and <ComponentB> at same position resets their state
To preserve state across type changes: lift state up or use a state manager

topics.title: The key Prop — Deep Mechanics
points: Key tells React "this specific component instance should be identified by this key, not by position"
Same key, same position: React treats it as same component → preserves state
Different key, same position: React unmounts old and mounts new → resets state entirely
Using index as key: works fine for static lists, BREAKS for reordered or filtered lists — React thinks old item 0 is still item 0, carries over wrong state
Correct key: must be stable, unique, and tied to the data item itself (e.g., item.id from database)
Power pattern: use key to force remount and reset state: <Input key={userId} /> — changing userId creates fresh Input
List diffing without keys: React tries position-based matching, cannot detect moves, produces unnecessary DOM operations
List diffing with keys: React detects moves, deletions, insertions correctly — only mutates what actually changed

topics.title: Component Identity & State Preservation
points: State is tied to component position in the tree, not to the component function reference
If same component function renders at same position → state is preserved even if props change
If component function changes (e.g., you define a component inside render) → position is same but React sees new type → unmounts and remounts → state lost
This is the exact reason never to define components inside other components' render

knowledge_check: React uses O(n) diffing not O(n³). What are the two assumptions that make this possible? What breaks if those assumptions are violated?
You have <Component condition={true}> and you change to <OtherComponent condition={false}> at the same tree position. What happens to the state of Component? Why?
You have a list of todos and you use index as key. A user deletes item at index 0. What does React incorrectly do? Draw the before/after DOM state.
You want to reset a form Input component whenever the user changes. How do you use the key prop to force a remount without conditionals?
Why does defining a component inside another component's render function cause state to reset on every re-render of the parent?
What is "component identity" in React? Is it determined by the function reference, position in tree, or key prop?
A user has a <Counter> at position 0. You conditionally render <Counter> or <div> at that position. Describe exactly what happens to Counter's state in both cases.
React walks the fiber tree level by level (not across levels). Why does this constraint make the O(n) algorithm safe?
You have a keyed list [A, B, C]. You reorder to [C, A, B]. What DOM operations does React perform with keys? Without keys?
What is the reconciliation process for a component that renders from <input type="text"> to <input type="checkbox">? Same DOM node or different?

days.title: useState & useReducer Internals + Hook Rules

topics.title: How Hooks Are Stored (The Linked List)
points: Hooks are stored as a linked list on the fiber's memoizedState field
Each hook call = one node in the list: { memoizedState: currentValue, queue: updateQueue, next: nextHook }
On every render, React traverses this list in ORDER — this is why hook call order must be stable
If you add/remove a hook conditionally, the list order changes → wrong state is assigned to wrong hook → bugs
Hook rule "only call hooks at top level" is not just a style guide — it's a technical requirement of the linked list architecture

topics.title: useState Internal Mechanics
points: useState is implemented as useReducer with a built-in reducer: (state, action) => typeof action === "function" ? action(state) : action
Initial render: creates hook node, sets memoizedState to initial value, returns [state, dispatcher]
Update render: reads memoizedState from hook node, processes any pending updates in the queue
Batching: multiple setState calls in same event handler are queued, processed together, produce one render
State update is enqueued (not applied immediately). React processes the queue on next render.
Functional update form: setCount(prev => prev + 1) — React passes the LATEST queued state as prev, preventing stale closures
Why functional update is safer: in async code or callbacks, count may be stale, but prev is always the latest queued value
Object.is() check: if new state === old state, React bails out of re-render entirely

topics.title: useReducer — When and Why
points: useReducer(reducer, initialState) — pure function: (state, action) => newState

When to prefer useReducer over useState: multiple related pieces of state that change together, complex state transition logic, state update depends on multiple previous values, testing state logic in isolation
Reducer must be pure: no side effects, no async, no API calls — just compute new state
dispatch is stable: same reference across renders — safe to pass to children without useCallback
Dispatch is synchronous: action is queued, state is NOT updated immediately, new state available on next render
Initializer function (3rd argument): useReducer(reducer, {}, init) — init(3rd arg) runs once, avoids expensive computation on every render

knowledge_check: Draw a diagram of the hooks linked list for a component with: useState(0), useEffect(fn,[]), useState("hello"). Show what each node contains.
Why can't you call a hook inside an if statement? Explain specifically what breaks in the linked list data structure.
You have: const [count, setCount] = useState(0). Inside a setTimeout you call setCount(count + 1) three times. How many times does count increment? Why? Fix it.
What does the functional update form setCount(prev => prev + 1) do differently from setCount(count + 1)? When does this matter?
Why is React's dispatch function from useReducer safe to include in dependency arrays without useCallback? How is this different from setState?
Your reducer receives action {type: "TOGGLE"} and you do state.active = !state.active inside it. What goes wrong? Why must reducers be pure?
useReducer vs useState: you're building a form with 8 fields, validation state, submission loading, and error messages. Which do you use and why?
What is the lazy initializer pattern in useReducer? When would you use it vs just passing the initial state directly?
React bails out of a re-render if new state === old state via Object.is(). You call setState with the same object reference (mutated). Does React re-render? Why?
On the very first render, how does React know which hook in the linked list corresponds to which useState call? What information does it use?

days.title: useEffect Deep Dive + useLayoutEffect + Cleanup

topics.title: What useEffect Actually Does
points: "useEffect lets you synchronize a component with an external system." — React docs. Not "do stuff after render."
External systems: browser APIs, third-party libraries, network, timers, subscriptions, WebSockets
Run timing: effect runs AFTER React commits to DOM and AFTER the browser paints
Effect runs after mount, then after every re-render where dependencies changed
Cleanup runs BEFORE next effect runs and BEFORE component unmounts

topics.title: Dependency Array Deep Mechanics
points: React compares deps using Object.is() (same as ===, except NaN === NaN)
No array: effect runs after EVERY render — rarely what you want
Empty array []: effect runs only after mount, cleanup only on unmount
[dep1, dep2]: effect runs after mount and whenever dep1 or dep2 changes by reference
Object in deps: { user } changes reference every render (new object each render) → infinite loop
Fix: destructure to primitives { user.id, user.name } which are stable primitives
Function in deps: inline function creates new reference each render → infinite loop if in deps
Fix: useCallback to stabilize function reference, or move function inside effect
Linter rule react-hooks/exhaustive-deps: ALWAYS follow it. If it says add X to deps, add it. If that causes bugs, fix the architecture.

topics.title: Race Conditions & Cleanup in Async Effects
points: Problem: user types "r" → fetch starts → user types "re" → second fetch starts → second fetch may resolve BEFORE first → stale data displayed
Solution 1: Boolean flag — let ignore = false; return () => { ignore = true; } — check if(!ignore) before setting state
Solution 2: AbortController — const controller = new AbortController(); fetch(url, {signal: controller.signal}); return () => controller.abort()
AbortController is preferred: actually cancels the network request, not just ignores the result
Never make useEffect async directly: async function returns a Promise, React expects the cleanup function — Promise is not a function
Pattern: define async function inside effect, then call it — const fetchData = async () => {...}; fetchData();

topics.title: useLayoutEffect — When & Why
points: useLayoutEffect fires synchronously AFTER DOM mutation but BEFORE browser paint
useEffect fires asynchronously AFTER browser paint
Use useLayoutEffect when: reading DOM measurements (element size, position) that affect what you render next → prevents visual flicker
Use useLayoutEffect for: tooltips that need to calculate position, scroll restoration, animations that need DOM position
Warning: useLayoutEffect in SSR causes React to warn — use useEffect or dynamically import the component
Default to useEffect. Use useLayoutEffect only when visual flicker is actually occurring and you've confirmed the timing difference fixes it.

knowledge_check: Why should you never make a useEffect callback async? Show the wrong code, explain what React receives, and show the correct pattern.
Write an async data fetching effect that handles race conditions using AbortController. Explain why AbortController is better than an "ignore" flag.
What happens if you put an object directly in the useEffect dependency array? Why does it cause an infinite loop? Fix it.
Your useEffect has a function dependency. The function is created inline in the component. What happens? Provide 2 ways to fix it.
Explain the exact sequence of events: component renders, DOM is updated, browser paints, useLayoutEffect fires, useEffect fires. Which of these fire synchronously?
When should you use useLayoutEffect instead of useEffect? Give a concrete UI example where using useEffect instead would cause visible flickering.
What does the cleanup function in useEffect do and WHEN does it run? Write a WebSocket subscription with proper cleanup.
You have useEffect with [] — runs once. But inside it you call a function that uses state. The function always sees the initial state value. Why? Fix it.
What is the "exhaustive-deps" ESLint rule? Should you ever disable it with eslint-disable? What should you do instead?
A component mounts, then state changes 3 times. In what order do effects and cleanups run across these 4 renders? List them in sequence.

days.title: useMemo, useCallback, React.memo — When They Help and When They Hurt

topics.title: Why React Re-Renders
points: React re-renders when: 1) State changes. 2) Context value changes. 3) Parent re-renders (by default, EVERY child re-renders).
Re-render ≠ DOM update: React re-renders (calls the function), diffs the output, only updates DOM if output changed
So most re-renders are actually cheap — the function runs again but DOM stays unchanged
Premature optimization with memo is harmful: memo adds comparison cost. If component is cheap, the comparison may cost more than just re-rendering.
When optimization IS worth it: component is genuinely expensive (complex calculations, large subtrees), confirmed by React DevTools Profiler

topics.title: React.memo Deep Mechanics
points: Wraps component, performs shallow comparison of props before re-rendering
Shallow comparison: compares each prop with Object.is() — primitives compared by value, objects/arrays/functions compared by reference
If all props are same reference → skip re-render
CRITICAL: If parent passes an object/array/function as prop without memoizing it, it creates NEW reference every parent render → React.memo is bypassed every time
Custom comparator: React.memo(Component, (prevProps, nextProps) => areEqual) — return true to skip re-render
React.memo does NOT help with context changes or internal state changes — only prop changes

topics.title: useMemo — Memoize Expensive Computations
points: const value = useMemo(() => expensiveCalc(a, b), [a, b])
Skips recalculation unless a or b changes
Also used to stabilize object/array references passed to memoized children: const options = useMemo(() => ({size: "large"}), []) — same reference every render
Measure BEFORE using useMemo — most computations are cheap (microseconds). useMemo itself has overhead.
Good candidate for useMemo: filtering/sorting 10,000+ items, expensive math, derived data used in many places
Bad candidate: simple string concatenation, conditional logic, any computation under 1ms

topics.title: useCallback — Stabilize Function References
points: const fn = useCallback(() => doSomething(a), [a]) — returns same function reference unless a changes
Primary use case: function passed as prop to memoized child component — without useCallback, child re-renders every time
Secondary use case: function in dependency array of another hook
useCallback(fn, deps) is equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps)
Anti-pattern: wrapping every function in useCallback "just to be safe" — adds overhead with zero benefit if function isn't passed to memoized children

knowledge_check: You wrap a component in React.memo but it still re-renders every time the parent renders. What are 3 possible reasons? How do you diagnose each?
What is the difference between useMemo and useCallback? Show they are equivalent using only useMemo.
You have a list of 50,000 items. Parent state changes on every keystroke. The list is wrapped in React.memo. The parent passes filter function as prop. Does memo help? Why not? Fix it.
When is React.memo harmful to use? Give a specific example where wrapping a component in memo makes performance WORSE.
useMemo vs external caching: you have an expensive calc in a component. Should you use useMemo or cache the result outside the component? When does each apply?
You use useMemo to memoize an object: const opts = useMemo(() => ({color}), [color]). But color is a string. Could you achieve the same with no memo? What's different?
Open React DevTools Profiler, record an interaction. How do you read the flame graph? What does each bar represent? How do you identify the problematic component?
Write a SearchResults component that renders 10,000 filtered items. Implement it correctly using React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback where appropriate and ONLY where appropriate.
What is the "render vs commit" cost model? Why does re-rendering not necessarily mean DOM mutation?

Your memoized child component uses a callback from the parent. The parent's state changes for an UNRELATED reason. Does the child re-render? What combination of React.memo + useCallback prevents this?

weeks.title: TypeScript Mastery + Advanced Hooks + State Architecture
weeks.overview: This week will be the single biggest upgrade to your hirability. Your resume lists TypeScript but shows zero advanced usage. Senior roles at 20-35 LPA require advanced TS. This week you close that gap completely.

days.title: TypeScript: Generics, Conditional Types, Utility Types

topics.title: Generics (The Most Important TS Feature)
points: Generics: write one function/type that works with multiple types while preserving type information
Basic generic: function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; } — T is inferred from argument
Generic constraints: <T extends object> — T must be an object type
<T extends keyof U> — T must be a key of U
Generic with default: <T = string> — T defaults to string if not specified
Generic React components: type Props<T> = { items: T[]; renderItem: (item: T) => ReactNode }
Real use: custom hook return types, API response wrappers, reusable data structures
Generic inference: TypeScript infers T from usage in most cases — prefer inference over explicit annotation

topics.title: Conditional Types
points: Syntax: T extends U ? X : Y — "if T is assignable to U, use type X, otherwise use type Y"
Use case: return type depends on input type
Distributive conditional types: when T is a union, condition distributes over each member
infer keyword: extract a type from within another type — infer R in return position
Example: type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never
Example: type UnwrapPromise<T> = T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T
NonNullable<T>: T extends null | undefined ? never : T

topics.title: Utility Types (Use These Daily)
points: Partial<T>: makes all properties optional — great for update payloads
Required<T>: makes all properties required
Readonly<T>: makes all properties readonly — great for state
Pick<T, K>: select subset of properties — Pick<User, "id" | "name">
Omit<T, K>: remove properties — Omit<User, "password"> for API responses
Record<K, V>: object with specific key and value types — Record<string, number>
Extract<T, U>: from union T, keep only what extends U
Exclude<T, U>: from union T, remove what extends U
ReturnType<T>: infer return type of a function
Parameters<T>: infer parameter types as tuple
Awaited<T>: unwrap Promise type recursively

topics.title: Discriminated Unions (The Pattern Senior Devs Love)
points: Discriminated union: union of types, each with a common literal property (the discriminant)
type Result<T> = { status: "success"; data: T } | { status: "error"; error: string } | { status: "loading" }
TypeScript narrows type based on discriminant in switch/if blocks — fully type-safe
Exhaustiveness check: add a default case with assertNever() to ensure all union members are handled
Perfect for: API state (loading/success/error), action types in reducers, form states
Alternative to optional chaining hell: instead of result.data?.user?.name, use discriminated union narrowing

knowledge_check: Write a generic useFetch<T> hook that returns { data: T | null, loading: boolean, error: string | null }. The component using it should have full type safety on data.
What is the infer keyword? Write a type that extracts the element type from any array type: ArrayElement<string[]> = string.
Write a type-safe API client function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<User> using generics and conditional types for error handling.
Create a discriminated union for async operation state: idle, loading, success<T>, error. Write a component that uses it with full type narrowing.
What is the difference between Pick and Omit? When would you use each? Give a real API response example.
Write a generic component <List<T>> that accepts items: T[] and renderItem: (item: T) => ReactNode with correct TypeScript types.
What is an exhaustiveness check? Write an assertNever function and show it catching a missing union member at compile time.
Explain distributive conditional types: why does (string | number) extends string ? true : false evaluate to boolean instead of false?
You receive an API response that may be User | Admin | SuperAdmin. Each has different fields. How do you model this with discriminated unions and handle each case safely?
What is the ReturnType utility type? Write your own version of it without using the built-in, using only conditional types and infer.

days.title: Advanced Custom Hooks + Custom Hook Design Patterns

topics.title: Custom Hook Design Principles
points: A custom hook is just a function that starts with "use" and calls other hooks — no magic
Single responsibility: one hook does one thing. useSearch is different from useDebounce.
Composition over complexity: build complex hooks from simple hooks
Return value design: return arrays ([value, setter]) when used like useState; objects ({data, loading, error}) when multiple named values
Type the return: always provide TypeScript types for your custom hooks

topics.title: Production-Grade Hook Library to Build
points: useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay: number): T — debounce any value change
useThrottle<T>(value: T, limit: number): T — throttle value updates
useLocalStorage<T>(key: string, initial: T): [T, Setter<T>] — persists to localStorage with cross-tab sync
usePrevious<T>(value: T): T | undefined — stores previous render's value via ref
useMediaQuery(query: string): boolean — responsive without CSS-only
useIntersectionObserver(ref, options): entry — lazy loading, infinite scroll
useEventListener(event, handler, target) — safe event listener with cleanup
useClickOutside(ref, handler) — dropdown/modal close on outside click
useAsync<T>(asyncFn, deps): AsyncState<T> — async operation with loading/error/data
useLatest<T>(value: T): RefObject<T> — always-fresh value ref (no stale closures)
useInterval(callback, delay) — safe setInterval with cleanup
useToggle(initial): [boolean, toggle] — common pattern extracted

knowledge_check: Write useDebounce from scratch with full TypeScript types. Explain every line including why cleanup cancels the timeout.
Write useAsync that accepts an async function and dependency array, returns {data, loading, error}, and handles cancellation.
Write useLocalStorage with cross-tab synchronization using the storage event. Handle JSON parse errors gracefully.
Why does usePrevious need useRef instead of useState? What would happen if you used useState? Would it cause bugs?
Write useIntersectionObserver and use it to build a lazy-loading image component that only fetches when visible.
What is the "useLatest" pattern? When does it solve stale closure issues better than adding to dependency arrays?
Design the public API (types/signature) for a useWebSocket hook. What should it return? What options should it accept? What does cleanup look like?
Write useClickOutside. Handle the case where clicking inside a portal (like a tooltip) should NOT trigger the outside click.
How do you test a custom hook? Write a test for useDebounce using React Testing Library's renderHook.
You have useSearch that debounces input and calls an API. It uses useDebounce and useAsync. Show how to compose them into one hook.

days.title: Context API Optimization + State Architecture Design

topics.title: How Context Causes Re-Renders
points: When context value changes, ALL components subscribed with useContext re-render — regardless of which part of the value they use
If context value is an object and parent passes new object every render → all consumers re-render every time
Fix: memoize context value with useMemo — const value = useMemo(() => ({user, logout}), [user, logout])
Better fix: split contexts — AuthContext (user) separate from AuthActionsContext (logout, login) — actions are stable, only user data changes
Context selectors (manual): use separate contexts for separate concerns, consumer only subscribes to what it needs

topics.title: Context + useReducer Pattern
points: Pattern: one context for state, one for dispatch — dispatch never changes, only state changes
Components that only dispatch actions subscribe to dispatch context → never re-render when state changes
This is the "poor man's Redux" pattern built into React
Scale limit: fine for medium apps, breaks down when you need middleware, devtools, or cross-context coordination

topics.title: State Colocation Principle
points: Rule: Keep state as close to where it's used as possible
Global state is expensive (causes more re-renders). Local state is cheap.
Before reaching for global state: can this live in the component? In the parent? In a common ancestor?
Anti-pattern: putting everything in a global store because it's "easier" — this kills performance and testability
Colocation with TanStack Query: server state doesn't need to be in global store — let React Query cache it and access it anywhere with the same query key

topics.title: Full State Architecture Decision Framework
points: useState: component-local UI state (modal open, active tab, form field)
useReducer: complex local state with multiple related fields
Context: truly global UI state (theme, user auth, locale) — max 2-3 contexts
Zustand: shared non-server state that doesn't fit in Context without re-render pain
TanStack Query: ALL server state — it handles caching, refetching, loading, errors
URL params: state that should be shareable, bookmarkable, or survive page refresh (filters, search, pagination)
React Hook Form: form state — do NOT use useState for forms with 3+ fields

knowledge_check: You have a UserContext that provides {user, theme, notifications, cart}. What is the performance problem? Redesign it.
Implement the Context + useReducer pattern with split state/dispatch contexts. Show a component that only dispatches and never re-renders from state changes.
When should you use Zustand over Context? Give 3 specific scenarios.
What is state colocation? Give an example of state that was wrongly put in a global store and show how to move it to be colocated.
Your auth user state is in Context. Your shopping cart is server-side (React Query). A component needs both. How do you access both without over-subscribing?
Explain why URL state is often the right answer for filter and search state. What does the user experience benefit?
You memoize a context value: const val = useMemo(() => ({count, increment}), [count, increment]). increment is defined inline. Does memoization work? Why not? Fix it.
Describe a full state architecture for an e-commerce app with: auth, cart (server-side), product filters, active modal, and theme.
What are the performance limits of React Context? At what point should you migrate from Context to Zustand?
What is "context selector" and why doesn't React have it built in? How do you simulate it with split contexts?

days.title: React Hook Form + Server State with TanStack Query

topics.title: Why Controlled Forms Are Wrong for Complex Forms
points: Controlled form: every keystroke → state update → re-render → entire form re-renders
10-field form, user types 100 characters = 100 * renders of entire form = 1000+ render cycles
React Hook Form: uncontrolled inputs, reads values only on submit, validates on blur/change without re-rendering
Result: 0 extra renders per keystroke, same user experience, dramatically better performance

topics.title: React Hook Form Deep Patterns
points: register: connects input to RHF without making it controlled
handleSubmit: wraps your submit handler, prevents default, validates before calling
formState: { errors, isDirty, isSubmitting, isValid, touchedFields, dirtyFields }
watch: subscribes to field value — causes re-render. Use sparingly. Use getValues() for non-reactive reads.
Controller: for custom inputs that aren't native HTML inputs (custom select, date picker, etc.)
useFieldArray: dynamic field arrays — add/remove/move items with correct validation
Validation: built-in rules (required, min, max, pattern), or integrate with Zod via zodResolver
Async validation: validate field against API (check username availability) — handle loading state
Multi-step form: use one form across steps with setValue and getValues

topics.title: TanStack Query Deep Patterns
points: Query keys as cache identifiers: ["users", userId] — always include dynamic variables that affect the query
staleTime: how long data is "fresh" before background refetch. Default: 0 (always stale). Set to 5min for reference data.
gcTime (was cacheTime): how long unused data stays in cache before garbage collected
Optimistic updates: mutate cache immediately on mutation, rollback on error — instant UI feedback
Invalidation: queryClient.invalidateQueries(["users"]) — marks data stale, triggers refetch
Prefetching: queryClient.prefetchQuery() — fetch data before user navigates to a page
useInfiniteQuery: for pagination and infinite scroll — manages pages array, hasNextPage, fetchNextPage
useSuspenseQuery: integrates with React Suspense — component suspends while data loads
Parallel queries: run multiple useQuery calls, they run in parallel automatically
Dependent queries: enabled: !!userId — don't run query until dependency is available

knowledge_check: Build a registration form with RHF + Zod: name, email, password, confirmPassword (must match), username (async check). Show all validation patterns.
What is the difference between watch and getValues in RHF? When does each cause a re-render?
What is optimistic mutation in TanStack Query? Write an optimistic "like" button that updates the UI instantly and rolls back on error.
Explain query key structure. Why is ["users"] different from ["users", userId] and why does this matter for cache?
What is staleTime vs gcTime? Set up TanStack Query for: user profile data (rarely changes), feed data (changes often), reference data like countries (never changes).
Build an infinite scroll list using useInfiniteQuery. Handle loading more, loading state, and the end of data.
You need to prefetch user data when hovering over a profile link. Write the code using TanStack Query.
How do you handle a mutation that affects multiple query caches? You add a comment — invalidate both comments list and post (which shows comment count).
Write a useFieldArray example for a dynamic "add education" form where users can add multiple education entries.
What is useSuspenseQuery and how does it integrate with React Suspense? How does error handling work differently from regular useQuery?

days.title: Zustand + Jotai + State Management Mastery

topics.title: Zustand Architecture
points: createStore creates a store outside React — no Provider needed, just import and use
Store contains: state (data) + actions (functions that update state) together
useStore(selector): component subscribes only to selected slice — only re-renders when THAT slice changes
Selector performance: const bears = useStore(state => state.bears) — only re-renders when bears changes, not entire store
Shallow comparison: useShallow for selecting objects/arrays that would otherwise create new references
Middleware: persist (localStorage), devtools (Redux DevTools), immer (immutable updates)
Slicing large stores: split into slices for maintainability, combine with spread
When to use: shared non-server state that is too painful in Context (auth state, cart, user preferences, UI state that many components need)

topics.title: Jotai — Atomic State
points: Atom: smallest unit of state — const countAtom = atom(0)
useAtom(countAtom): [value, setter] — only re-renders component when THIS atom changes
Derived atoms: atom(get => get(priceAtom) * get(quantityAtom)) — computed from other atoms
Write atoms: atom(get => ..., set => ...) — custom read and write logic
Async atoms: atom(async get => { const res = await fetch(...); return res.json(); }) — integrates with Suspense
Fine-grained subscriptions: each component subscribes to exactly what it needs — no over-subscription
Provider scope: wrap part of app with Provider to give isolated atom state
When to use Jotai over Zustand: when you have many independent pieces of state that different components subscribe to independently

knowledge_check: Build a Zustand shopping cart with: addItem, removeItem, updateQuantity, clearCart, persist to localStorage. Use selectors to minimize re-renders.
What is the selector pattern in Zustand? Why is const x = useStore() (no selector) a performance anti-pattern?
What is useShallow in Zustand and when do you need it?
Build the same shopping cart with Jotai. Compare the mental model. When would you prefer Jotai's approach?
Zustand has middleware. What does the persist middleware do? What are edge cases when using it (e.g., state shape changes)?
You have a Zustand store with 20 fields. A component only needs 2 of them. Write the selector. What happens if you don't use a selector?
Explain derived atoms in Jotai. Build a cart total atom derived from items atom and discount atom.
Final decision framework: you're building a SaaS dashboard. List every piece of state and assign it to: useState, Context, Zustand, TanStack Query, or URL params. Justify each.
What is the difference between Jotai atoms at module scope vs inside a component? What is Provider in Jotai and why would you use it?
How do you use Redux DevTools with Zustand? How do you name store actions for better DevTools experience?

days.title: Testing React Apps Like a Senior Engineer
days.why_it_matters: You listed Jest/Vitest on your resume with zero test code shown anywhere. This will get you rejected at companies that care about quality. Learn this properly.

topics.title: Testing Philosophy
points: "Test behavior, not implementation" — Kent C. Dodds. Test what the user sees and does, not internal function calls.
Testing pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, few E2E tests
React Testing Library philosophy: query DOM like a user would. getByRole, getByText, not getByClassName.
Avoid testing implementation details: component internal state, which function was called, React internals
Test confidence: your tests should fail when the behavior breaks, not when you refactor internal implementation

topics.title: React Testing Library Core API
points: render(component): renders component into jsdom, returns queries
screen: query methods on the rendered output
getByRole: most accessible query — matches by ARIA role (button, textbox, heading, listitem)
getByText: matches visible text content
getByLabelText: finds form input by its label — mirrors how screen readers work
queryBy vs getBy: queryBy returns null if not found (use for asserting NOT present), getBy throws
findBy: async query, waits up to 1s for element to appear — for async operations
userEvent vs fireEvent: userEvent simulates real user interaction (type, click, tab), fireEvent is synthetic
waitFor: wait for any expectation to pass — for async state updates

topics.title: Testing Patterns
points: Testing hooks: renderHook from @testing-library/react
Mocking API calls: MSW (Mock Service Worker) — intercept at network level, most realistic
Testing async: findBy queries, waitFor, act()
Testing forms: userEvent.type, userEvent.click submit button, assert validation messages
Testing context: wrap component in context provider in render, or create custom renderWithProviders
Testing React Query: wrap with QueryClientProvider, use fresh QueryClient per test
Snapshot testing: use sparingly — captures DOM output, fails on any change. Better for stable UI components.
Accessibility testing: @testing-library/jest-dom + axe-core for automated a11y checks

knowledge_check: Write a test for a login form: user types email and password, clicks submit, see loading state, then success message. Mock the API with MSW.
What is the difference between getByRole and getByText? Which is preferred and why?
Write a test for your useDebounce hook using renderHook. Assert that the value doesn't update until delay ms have passed.
What is userEvent vs fireEvent? Why does the RTL docs recommend userEvent?
Write a test that asserts an element is NOT in the document. Which query do you use? Why?
How do you test a component that uses TanStack Query? What setup is required? Write an example.
What does act() do in React Testing Library? When do you need to wrap code in act()?
Write a test for an infinite scroll component that loads more items when user scrolls to bottom.
What is MSW and why is it better than mocking fetch directly? Set up MSW for a test suite.
What does "test behavior not implementation" mean? Give an example of a test that tests implementation (wrong) and one that tests behavior (right).

weeks.title: React Performance Engineering + Frontend System Design
weeks.overview: This is where you become worth 20+ LPA. Not because you know how to use React, but because you can make React fast AND design complex frontend systems.

days.title: Performance Measurement, Core Web Vitals, Profiling

topics.title: Core Web Vitals (Know These Cold)
points: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): time until largest visible element (image or text block) is rendered. Target: < 2.5s. Poor: > 4s.
INP (Interaction to Next Paint): replaces FID. Time from user interaction to next frame rendered. Target: < 200ms. Poor: > 500ms.
CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): unexpected visual shifts during page load. Target: < 0.1. Caused by: images without dimensions, ads, dynamic content insertion above existing content.
TTFB (Time to First Byte): time for browser to receive first byte from server. Not a Core Web Vital but important for SSR.
FCP (First Contentful Paint): when first content appears. Not a CWV but measures perceived load speed.

topics.title: React DevTools Profiler
points: Flame graph: each bar = one component. Width = time spent. Click bar to see why it rendered.
"Why did this render" section: shows which props/state/hooks changed to trigger render
Ranked chart: components sorted by render duration — spot slow components instantly
Record: click record, perform interaction, click stop — then analyze
Identify: unnecessarily re-rendering components (renders when parent renders without any prop change)
Highlight updates: enable "Highlight updates when components render" setting to see live re-renders

topics.title: Chrome Performance Tab
points: Long tasks: any script > 50ms shown in red — these block user interaction
FPS chart: dropped frames appear as red bars in top section
Call tree: see which functions take most time, expand to find the slow code
Bottom-up: find total time spent in each function across all call stacks
Layout and paint: large green blocks = expensive layout recalculations (often caused by reading then writing DOM)

knowledge_check: What are the 3 Core Web Vitals? What does each measure, what is the "good" threshold, and name one React-specific cause of a poor score for each.
Open a real website in Chrome DevTools, run a Lighthouse audit. Identify 3 issues. For each: what is the issue, what score does it affect, and how would you fix it in a React app?
Use React DevTools Profiler on a component with 3 children. Record a click interaction. Identify which child re-rendered unnecessarily and what caused it.
What causes CLS in a React app? Give 3 specific React patterns that cause layout shift.
What is INP (Interaction to Next Paint)? How does a heavy React re-render cause poor INP?
A user complains the app "feels laggy". Walk through your diagnostic process step by step — what tools, what metrics, what you look for.
What is a "long task" in browser performance? How does React's concurrent mode help reduce long tasks?
How do you measure LCP in a production React app with real user data (not just Lighthouse)?
What is TTFB and how does SSR with Next.js affect it compared to a pure client-side React app?
What does "highlight updates when components render" in React DevTools show you? Run it on your portfolio app and find 3 unnecessary renders.

days.title: Rendering Optimization: memo, Virtual Lists, Code Splitting

topics.title: Systematic Optimization Process
points: Rule 1: MEASURE FIRST. Never optimize without profiler evidence. "I think this is slow" is wrong 80% of the time.
Rule 2: Fix the most impactful problem first. Optimizing a component that renders in 0.3ms is wasted effort.
Rule 3: Measure after each change. Confirm the optimization actually helped.

Optimization priority order: 1) Fix architectural problems (wrong component structure), 2) Fix expensive calculations (useMemo), 3) Fix prop instability (useCallback, useMemo for objects), 4) Memoize components (React.memo), 5) Virtualize (only for large lists)

topics.title: List Virtualization Deep Dive
points: Problem: rendering 10,000 list items = 10,000 DOM nodes = huge memory and layout recalculation cost
Solution: render only visible items (typically 10-30) + a buffer above and below viewport
Technique: absolutely position items, translate them into view as user scrolls
react-window: simpler, fixed-size and variable-size list/grid components
TanStack Virtual (react-virtual): more flexible, handles variable heights, horizontal scrolling, grid
Key challenge: variable height items require height estimation or measurement before virtualization
Combining with infinite scroll: TanStack Virtual + TanStack Query useInfiniteQuery is the production pattern

topics.title: Code Splitting Strategies
points: Route-based splitting: lazy load each route — most impactful, easiest to implement
Component-based splitting: lazy load large components (rich text editor, chart library, map)
Interaction-based splitting: load code only when user performs specific action (click a button that opens a complex modal)
Preloading: prefetch chunk when user hovers over link — balances initial load vs interaction speed
Bundle analysis: run next build && next analyze, or webpack-bundle-analyzer — identify large chunks
Third-party impact: moment.js = 67kb, date-fns = 2-12kb tree-shakeable. lodash = 71kb, lodash-es = tree-shakeable. Math: replace date-fns with date-fns/format for individual imports.
Dynamic import(): const { default: Chart } = await import("recharts") — code splits automatically

topics.title: Image & Font Optimization
points: WebP: 30% smaller than JPEG with same quality. AVIF: 50% smaller but less browser support. Use srcset for progressive enhancement.
loading="lazy": browser native lazy loading for images — add to all below-fold images
Prevent CLS: always set width and height on img elements — browser reserves space before image loads
Blur placeholder: generate blurred base64 thumbnail, show while full image loads — plaiceholder library or Next.js Image placeholder
font-display: swap — FOUT (flash of unstyled text) is better than FOIT (invisible text). Use swap.
Preload critical fonts: <link rel="preload" as="font"> for fonts used above the fold
Self-host fonts: Google Fonts = extra DNS lookup + network request. Self-hosting = one fewer blocking resource.

knowledge_check: You have a 10,000 item list. Implement it with TanStack Virtual. Show the container, the virtualizer setup, and the item rendering pattern.
Walk through the complete code splitting setup for a Next.js app with 8 routes. Include Suspense boundaries and loading fallbacks.
You have a recharts bundle that is 200kb. How do you code-split it so it only loads when the chart is visible? Show the React.lazy + Suspense implementation.
What is the systematic process to fix re-renders? A parent component re-renders and causes 5 children to unnecessarily re-render. Walk through fixing it step by step.
What causes layout thrashing? Why does reading DOM properties (offsetHeight) inside a loop before writing cause performance issues?
Explain font-display: swap vs block vs optional. Which do you use for body text vs icon fonts and why?
Your Lighthouse score shows LCP of 6 seconds. The LCP element is a hero image. What are 5 specific things you do to improve it?
What is the difference between React.lazy and dynamic import? Are they the same thing or different?
Implement a prefetch-on-hover strategy for navigation links in a Next.js app. Show the onMouseEnter handler.
You replace moment.js with date-fns. How do you verify tree shaking is actually working? What tools do you use?

days.title: React Concurrent Features: useTransition, useDeferredValue, Suspense

topics.title: The Problem Concurrent Mode Solves
points: Without Concurrent Mode: every state update renders synchronously, blocking main thread until done
Symptom: typing in a search box causes expensive list to re-render on every keystroke → input feels laggy
With Concurrent Mode: React can interrupt low-priority renders to process high-priority user input

topics.title: useTransition
points: const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition()
Wrapping a state update in startTransition marks it as "non-urgent" — React will defer it if needed
isPending: true while transition is pending — use to show loading indicator without blocking input
Use case: search — setQuery is urgent (input must feel instant), but the list filtering is non-urgent
React may run the transition render multiple times, keeping the previous committed state visible until done
Transition updates are interruptible: if user types again before transition finishes, React discards old transition and starts new one

topics.title: useDeferredValue
points: const deferredValue = useDeferredValue(value)
Defers updating the deferred value — shows stale value while new value is being rendered
Use case: expensive child component receives a prop that changes frequently
With React.memo: memoize expensive component, pass deferredValue as prop — component only re-renders when React has spare time
useDeferredValue vs useTransition: useDeferredValue is for when you receive a value from a parent and can't wrap the state setter in startTransition

topics.title: Suspense for Data Fetching
points: Suspense boundary: <Suspense fallback={<Loading/>}>children</Suspense>
When a child suspends (throws a Promise), Suspense shows the fallback instead
When the Promise resolves, Suspense shows the child
useSuspenseQuery (TanStack Query): component suspends while query is loading — no loading state needed in component
Nested Suspense: fine-grained loading states — each section has its own Suspense boundary
ErrorBoundary + Suspense: combine for complete async handling (loading → data OR error)

knowledge_check: Build a search component with useTransition: input updates instantly, filtered list shows isPending indicator, list only re-renders when transition completes.
What is the difference between useTransition and useDeferredValue? Give a concrete example of when you'd be forced to use useDeferredValue.
How does Suspense work mechanically? What does a component need to do to "suspend"? (Hint: it involves throwing a Promise)
Build a component tree with 3 nested Suspense boundaries. What happens when each section loads independently?
You have an expensive filtered list component. Parent search state changes on every keystroke. Show the complete solution using useDeferredValue + React.memo.
What does isPending from useTransition tell you? When is it true vs false?
How do you combine Suspense with an ErrorBoundary? Show the component structure for a data-fetching page that handles loading, success, and error states.
startTransition is available outside of React components (not the hook). When would you use startTransition instead of useTransition?
What happens if you wrap a setState in startTransition but the state controls a user input (controlled input)? What goes wrong?
Explain why Concurrent Mode makes render phase purity critical. What breaks if a component has side effects in its render function when React might run it multiple times?

days.title: Frontend System Design (Your Biggest Gap — Close It Now)
days.why_it_matters: You have ZERO frontend system design demonstrated. Senior roles — especially Architect and Principal — will ask you to design frontend systems. This is the section that gets you from 12 LPA to 25+ LPA.

topics.title: Frontend System Design Framework
points: Step 1: Clarify requirements (2 min): What is the scale? How many users? Real-time? Offline? Mobile?
Step 2: Component architecture — what are the major components and their hierarchy?
Step 3: State architecture — where does each piece of state live and why?
Step 4: Data flow — how does data move between components? Props down, events up? Context? State manager?
Step 5: API design — what endpoints does the frontend need? What data shape?
Step 6: Performance — what are the performance challenges and how do you solve them?
Step 7: Real-time concerns — does anything need live updates? WebSocket or polling?
Step 8: Error handling and edge cases

weeks.title: Advanced Patterns, Accessibility, Design Systems

topics.title: Advanced Component Patterns
points: Compound Component Pattern: <Select><Select.Option/><Select.Trigger/></Select> — components share implicit state via Context
Render Props: controlled inversion — component receives function as children: <Mouse>{({x,y}) => <Cursor x={x} y={y}/>}</Mouse>
Headless Components: provide behavior/state only, no UI — Radix UI is built on this. You control the styling.
Polymorphic components: <Button as="a" href="/"> — renders as anchor but has Button styles/behavior. Typed with TypeScript generics.
Controlled vs Uncontrolled API: let component manage its own state OR accept value + onChange for external control
Slots pattern (children composition): instead of props for everything, compose with children sub-components
Higher Order Components (HOC): rarely used in modern React but you must understand legacy codebases

topics.title: Accessibility (a11y) — Non-Negotiable for Senior Roles
points: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance: the standard for accessibility. Know: perceivable, operable, understandable, robust.
Semantic HTML: use <button> not <div onClick>. Use <nav>, <main>, <aside>, <header>, <footer>.
ARIA roles: when HTML semantics are not enough — role="dialog", role="listbox", role="combobox"
ARIA properties: aria-label, aria-labelledby, aria-describedby, aria-expanded, aria-selected, aria-hidden
Keyboard navigation: Tab (focus), Shift+Tab (back), Enter/Space (activate), Escape (close), Arrow keys (navigate within component)
Focus management: when modal opens, focus must move inside modal. When modal closes, focus returns to trigger.
Focus trap: while modal is open, Tab cycles only within modal — use focus-trap-react or Radix UI
Color contrast: text must have 4.5:1 contrast ratio (AA). Use browser DevTools accessibility panel to check.
Screen reader testing: VoiceOver (Mac), NVDA (Windows) — test at least one
axe-core: automated a11y testing in Jest/Cypress — catches ~30% of a11y issues automatically

topics.title: Design System Architecture
points: Design tokens: CSS variables or JS objects for colors, spacing, typography — single source of truth
Component API design: consistent naming, minimal required props, sensible defaults, full TypeScript types
Theming architecture: CSS variables for runtime switching, dark mode without JavaScript flash
Storybook: document components with stories, visual testing, interaction testing
Variant patterns: cva (class-variance-authority) for managing component variants with TypeScript safety
Radix UI as base: unstyled, accessible primitives — add your styling on top
shadcn/ui: pre-built components using Radix + Tailwind — understand the architecture, not just copy-paste

months.title: Next.js Mastery + Real-Time UI + Production Systems
months.objective: Month 1 made you a React expert. Month 2 makes you a production-ready Next.js specialist and real-time UI engineer. By end of Month 2, you can lead the frontend of any product from architecture to deployment. You'll have 3 portfolio projects that prove it.

weeks.title: Next.js App Router: Deep Architecture
weeks.overview: You used Next.js in PharmaSync. But you used it as a fancy React router. It's not. Next.js App Router is a completely different paradigm. Learn it properly.

days.title: App Router Architecture, RSC, Server vs Client Components

topics.title: The Fundamental Shift: Server Components
points: React Server Components (RSC): components that render ONLY on the server. Never hydrated. Never shipped to client.
Server components can: access databases directly, read files, access env variables, be async
Server components CANNOT: use useState, useEffect, event handlers, or any browser APIs
"use client" directive: marks a module boundary — everything in this file and its imports becomes client JS
Default in App Router: ALL components are server components by default. Add "use client" only when needed.
The boundary: server components can import client components but NOT the reverse (client components cannot import server components)
Children as props: pass server components as children to client components — they remain server components
Why RSC: dramatically reduces JavaScript sent to client (server components add ZERO JS to bundle)

topics.title: App Router File System Conventions
points: app/layout.tsx: shared layout wrapping children. Does NOT re-render on navigation.
app/page.tsx: route page component. Rendered for that specific URL.
app/loading.tsx: automatic Suspense boundary for the page. Shows while page fetches data.
app/error.tsx: error boundary for the route segment. Must be a "use client" component.
app/not-found.tsx: rendered when notFound() is called or 404 occurs.
app/template.tsx: like layout but re-renders on navigation (for route-specific animations).
Route groups: (groupName)/page.tsx — group without affecting URL. Used for different layouts.
Parallel routes: @slot/page.tsx — render multiple pages in same layout simultaneously.
Intercepting routes: (.)route — intercept navigation and show in modal while URL changes.
Dynamic segments: [id]/page.tsx, [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all), [[...slug]]/page.tsx (optional catch-all)

topics.title: Data Fetching in App Router
points: Fetch in server components: next/fetch with built-in caching — fetch(url, { cache: "force-cache" | "no-store" })
cache: "force-cache" (default): cached indefinitely, like SSG
cache: "no-store": fetches fresh data on every request, like SSR
revalidate: fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 60 } }) — cached for 60 seconds, like ISR
React cache(): memoize data fetches within a single request — prevents duplicate fetches across components
Parallel data fetching: const [user, posts] = await Promise.all([getUser(), getPosts()]) — faster than sequential
Sequential fetching: when one fetch depends on the result of another — unavoidable but minimize
generateStaticParams: for dynamic routes — tell Next.js which params to pre-render at build time

topics.title: Server Actions
points: "use server" directive: marks a function that runs on the server. Can be called from client components.
Form actions: <form action={serverAction}> — works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement)
useFormState: hook that wraps a server action to get form state and previous result
useFormStatus: get pending state of nearest form submission — shows loading without useState
After mutation: call revalidatePath() or revalidateTag() to invalidate cached data
Security: server actions are POST requests under the hood — Next.js adds CSRF protection automatically
Error handling: throw errors in server actions, catch with error.tsx boundary

knowledge_check: What is a React Server Component? List 5 things it CAN do and 5 things it CANNOT do compared to a client component.

generateStaticParams is called at build time for dynamic routes. What does it return? What happens for params that are NOT in the returned array?

days.title: SSG, SSR, ISR, Edge Runtime, Caching

topics.title: Rendering Strategies Deep Dive
points: Static Site Generation (SSG): HTML generated at BUILD TIME. Fastest for users. Served from CDN. Stale for dynamic content.
Server-Side Rendering (SSR): HTML generated on SERVER on each REQUEST. Always fresh. Slower TTFB. Good for authenticated, personalized content.
Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR): Static HTML + revalidation interval. Best of both worlds for semi-static content.
Streaming SSR: HTML streams from server as each Suspense boundary resolves. User sees content progressively — no waiting for ALL data.
Partial Pre-rendering (PPR): experimental Next.js feature — static shell served instantly from CDN, dynamic holes filled via streaming.

topics.title: Next.js Caching Architecture (4 Layers)
points: Request Memoization: React cache() — within a SINGLE SERVER RENDER, same URL fetch is deduplicated
Data Cache: persistent across requests and deployments — fetch() responses cached on server. Invalidated by revalidatePath/revalidateTag.
Full Route Cache: entire rendered HTML cached on server for static routes — like CDN cache on the server
Router Cache (Client): client-side cache of route segments — prefetched pages and recently visited pages cached in browser
When to opt out: dynamic routes, authenticated content, frequently changing data — use cache: "no-store" or dynamic = "force-dynamic"

topics.title: Edge Runtime
points: Edge Runtime: runs on CDN nodes globally — not Node.js, it's a V8 Isolate (like Cloudflare Workers)
Ultra-low latency: code runs at the CDN edge closest to the user
Limitations: no Node.js APIs, no file system, limited npm packages, no native modules
Use for: middleware (auth redirects, A/B testing, header modification), edge API routes
export const runtime = "edge" in layout/page to opt a route into edge runtime
Middleware: middleware.ts at root — runs on EVERY request before routing. Use for auth, redirects, internationalization.

knowledge_check: Explain the 4 caching layers in Next.js. For each: what is cached, where, for how long, and how do you invalidate it?
When should you use SSG vs SSR vs ISR? Give a real-world example for each (e.g., a specific page in an e-commerce app).
What is streaming SSR? How does it improve perceived performance? How do you implement it with Suspense in Next.js?

revalidatePath vs revalidateTag: what is the difference? When would you use tags over paths?
You have a product detail page. Product data changes occasionally. Reviews change frequently. Comments change in real-time. How do you design the caching strategy for this page?
What is Partial Pre-rendering (PPR)? How does it differ from ISR? What problem does it solve?

weeks.title: Real-Time UI Engineering (Your Current Weak Spot)
weeks.overview: You listed WebSockets on your resume. You used webhooks and server-sent events. That's not real-time UI engineering. Real-time UI is about managing live state in the browser.

days.title: WebSocket State Management, Reconnection, Connection Lifecycle

topics.title: WebSocket Connection Lifecycle
points: States: CONNECTING (0), OPEN (1), CLOSING (2), CLOSED (3)
Handshake: HTTP Upgrade request → 101 Switching Protocols → bidirectional TCP connection
Events: onopen, onmessage, onerror, onclose
onclose: has code and reason — codes 1000 (normal), 1001 (going away), 1006 (abnormal), 1011 (server error)
React integration: create WebSocket in useEffect, close in cleanup, store instance in useRef (not state — you don't want re-renders from WS object)

topics.title: Reconnection Strategy (Production Must-Have)
points: Networks are unreliable. You must implement reconnection for ANY production WebSocket.
Exponential backoff: 1s → 2s → 4s → 8s → 16s → 30s (cap) — prevents overwhelming server on mass reconnect
Jitter: add random offset to backoff — prevents "thundering herd" (all clients reconnecting simultaneously after outage)
Max attempts: after N failed attempts, stop reconnecting and show user a message to refresh
Reconnect on: abnormal closure (code 1006), network error. Do NOT reconnect on: intentional close (code 1000), auth error.
Reconnect state in React: { status: "connected" | "connecting" | "disconnected" | "error", retryCount }
On reconnect: re-subscribe to rooms/channels, request any missed events (server-side with lastEventId or timestamp)

topics.title: Message Protocol & State Synchronization
points: Protocol design: {type: "message_type", payload: {...}, id: "uuid", timestamp: number}
Optimistic UI: apply changes locally immediately, server confirms → keep. Server rejects → rollback.
Event sourcing pattern: server sends events (ITEM_ADDED, ITEM_UPDATED, ITEM_DELETED), client applies to local state
Handling missed messages: reconnect → send lastSeenEventId → server sends all events since that ID
Message deduplication: server assigns IDs to events, client ignores already-processed IDs
State divergence: periodic "sync" poll as safety net — if WebSocket state diverges from server, poll overwrites

topics.title: useWebSocket Custom Hook Architecture
points: Connection management: create, reconnect, cleanup in one hook
Message subscription: callback pattern — onMessage handler per message type
Send API: send(message) queues if not connected, sends when connection restored
Status exposure: { status, lastMessage, send, reconnect, disconnect }
Singleton pattern: one WebSocket connection shared across components using Context or Zustand
Room/channel concept: single WS connection, multiple logical channels via message routing

knowledge_check: Implement useWebSocket hook with: connection lifecycle, exponential backoff reconnection with jitter, message queue for offline messages, and typed message protocol.

days.title: Real-Time UI Patterns: Chat, Live Collaboration, Presence

topics.title: Real-Time Chat System Architecture
points: Message list: virtualized (react-virtual), reverse chronological, maintains scroll position
Scroll behavior: stay at bottom when new message arrives IF user is already at bottom. Don't scroll if user has scrolled up to read history.
"Scroll to bottom" button: appears when user scrolls up, disappears when at bottom
Message states: sending → sent → delivered → read (with timestamps)
Typing indicators: debounced "user is typing" events (500ms debounce to prevent spam)
Message grouping: consecutive messages from same user within 2 minutes = one group, fewer timestamps/avatars
Optimistic send: show message immediately, gray/italic until server confirms
Failed messages: show retry option for failed sends — keep in local state until confirmed
Pagination: load last 50 messages on open. Load older messages when user scrolls to top (prepend, maintain scroll position)

topics.title: Presence System (Who's Online)
points: Presence: real-time list of who is currently viewing or active
Heartbeat: client sends "ping" every 30s. Server marks user offline after 90s with no heartbeat.
Join/leave events: broadcast to room when user connects or disconnects
React state: Map<userId, PresenceData> — fast lookup by userId
Cursor presence (Figma-style): throttle cursor events to 30fps (every 33ms), broadcast position
Idle detection: user inactive for 5min → mark as "away"
Scale: for large rooms, use server-aggregated presence instead of broadcasting every join/leave

topics.title: Optimistic UI — Production Patterns
points: useOptimistic (React 19/Next.js 14): optimistically update state while action is pending
Rollback strategy: keep original state, on error → restore original. Show error toast.
ID generation: optimistic items need a temporary client-side ID — use crypto.randomUUID() or nanoid
Replace temp ID: when server confirms, replace temporary ID with server-assigned ID
Conflict detection: if server rejects because item was deleted by another user, show specific error
Offline queue: queue mutations while offline, replay when reconnected, handle conflicts

knowledge_check: Build a real-time chat: message list (virtualized), typing indicator, optimistic send, scroll-to-bottom button, message grouping.

A user has 2 browser tabs open. They make a change in tab 1. How does tab 2 update in real-time? Is there one WebSocket per tab or shared?
Build a "read receipts" system: show when each message was read by each participant. What events does the server need to send?
How do you prevent a WebSocket event storm when 1000 users connect simultaneously after a server restart? Show rate limiting and backpressure strategies.
